planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; Florida anise is a reliable, low-maintenance native shrub for shaded, acidic woodland conditions in the Gulf South.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in late summer or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Florida anise is a southeastern native evergreen shrub that is one of the very best shade-tolerant shrubs for Gulf Coast and Southeast landscapes, producing handsome, lance-shaped, glossy dark-green leaves that release a strong anise-star fragrance when bruised or crushed. Unusual dark maroon-red, multi-petaled starfish-like flowers appear in spring and sporadically through the year. All parts of the plant are poisonous and should not be confused with the edible Asian star anise (Illicium verum). Provides excellent year-round screening in shaded areas and is notably deer-resistant due to its aromatic foliage. Tolerates wet soils seasonally.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, average to humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Carolina allspice performs best with afternoon shade in hot summer climates.

directions: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Carolina allspice is a native eastern North American deciduous shrub prized for its extraordinary fragrance — the strappy, dark reddish-brown flowers that appear from late spring through summer emit a powerful, spicy, strawberry-banana-wine aroma that is one of the most complex and pleasing scents in the garden. The flowers are unusual and somewhat primitive-looking, with many overlapping tepals. The bark and leaves are also aromatic when bruised. Plants thrive in woodland gardens and shaded borders, tolerating a wide range of soil conditions including clay. 'Athens' (chartreuse-yellow flowers) and 'Michael Lindsey' (deep maroon, strong fragrance) are notable cultivars. Suckers freely to form dense colonies.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in late spring or plant container-grown specimens on a trellis, fence, or through shrubs in moist, well-drained, average, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Carolina jessamine is the state flower of South Carolina and one of the finest native vines for the Southeast.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens on a trellis, fence, arbor, or mailbox post in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Carolina jessamine is an evergreen, twining native vine that produces a spectacular display of bright, golden-yellow, trumpet-shaped, sweetly fragrant flowers in late winter to early spring — often the first vine to bloom, sometimes as early as January in zone 9. The glossy, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves are handsome year-round. The vine grows vigorously to 10–20 feet and can be allowed to cascade down banks, scramble through shrubs, or climb structures. All parts of the plant are highly toxic — do not confuse with true jasmines and never allow children to put flowers in their mouths. Valuable early-spring nectar source for native bees and hummingbirds returning from winter.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; chaste tree demands excellent drainage and thrives in hot, dry conditions that defeat many ornamentals.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; chaste tree is a fast-growing, multi-stemmed deciduous large shrub or small tree with deeply fragrant, spicy gray-green palmately compound leaves — a strong, pleasant sage-and-lavender scent — and tall, upright spikes of lavender-blue flowers from summer through fall that make it one of the most valuable summer-blooming ornamentals for hot, dry climates. It blooms profusely on new growth; cutting back hard in late winter to 12–18 inches above ground produces vigorous new shoots and the best flower display. The dried fruits were historically used as a spice and medicinal herb (the name 'chaste tree' refers to its historical use to reduce libido). 'Shoal Creek' (large, deep blue spikes) and 'Abbeville Blue' are outstanding selections.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in spring or fall; glossy abelia is among the longest-blooming shrubs in the Southeast, flowering from late spring through fall frost.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; glossy abelia is a semi-evergreen hybrid shrub that ranks among the most reliably long-blooming ornamentals for the Southeast and Gulf South, producing masses of small, funnel-shaped, white to blush-pink, pleasantly fragrant flowers continuously from late spring through hard frost — a bloom period of 5–6 months that few shrubs match. The small, glossy, bronze-tinged leaves are attractive year-round in mild climates. Hummingbirds and butterflies visit the flowers consistently. It is highly adaptable to soil conditions, tolerating heat, humidity, and urban pollution. Prune selectively in late winter to remove oldest canes and encourage vigorous, floriferous new growth. 'Kaleidoscope' (variegated yellow and green) and 'Confetti' (variegated pink-white) are popular selections.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; bear's breeches tolerates drought and dry shade once established but is invasive by deep root pieces — remove carefully and completely.

directions: Plant divisions or container specimens in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in partial shade to full sun — afternoon shade is preferred in hot summer climates; bear's breeches is grown for its exceptionally bold, deeply lobed and cut, glossy, dark-green leaves up to 24 inches long that have inspired architectural ornamentation since ancient Greece (the Corinthian column capital is modeled on acanthus leaves). Tall spikes of white and purple-hooded flowers rise 4–5 feet in late spring to early summer. The plant is drought-tolerant and adaptable once established but the deep, fleshy roots make it difficult to remove — a single broken root regenerates into a new plant. Excellent as a dramatic accent in formal gardens, dry shade borders, and Mediterranean-style landscapes. Goes dormant in extreme heat; re-emerges in cool weather.
